14	TEA tests, traceability matrix entries, all four test tiers green, deployed
15	to a `dev` GAS deployment via clasp, E2E tests passing against the live
16	spreadsheet.
17	
18	This phase is the **load test** for the architecture. Any pain point
19	discovered here results in an ADR amendment, not a workaround.
20	
21	## Entry criteria
22	
23	- PHASE-4 exit gate passed.
24	- Product side has produced (in a separate PR chain):
25	  `openspec/vision.md`, ≥ 1 brief, ≥ 1 PRD section, ≥ 1 Epic with coverage,
26	  ≥ 1 Story.
27	
28	## Scope
29	
30	1. `projects/<name>/` with full layout per
31	   [ADR-0001](../decisions/ADR-0001-monorepo-layout.md) and
32	   [ADR-0003](../decisions/ADR-0003-appscript-runtime.md).
33	2. `.clasp.json` pointing at a `dev` GAS deployment owned by a CI service
34	   account.
35	3. Real adapter wiring against shared-ts.
36	4. **Per-project BMAD output isolation** per
37	   [`ADR-0002` §9](../decisions/ADR-0002-bmad-integration.md):
38	   - Author `projects/<name>/_bmad/custom/config.toml` overriding
39	     `bmm.{planning,implementation,project_knowledge}` to point under
40	     `projects/<name>/openspec/_bmad-output/{planning,implementation,knowledge}`.
41	   - Document the per-project working-directory convention (sessions
42	     working on a project run with CWD = `projects/<name>/`).
43	   - Verify the BMAD resolver picks up the override by invoking a BMAD
44	     skill from the project CWD and asserting output landed under the
45	     project's staging dir, not the repo-level one. The first project
46	     sets the precedent for all later projects.
47	4. Test suite across all four tiers, each red-first.
48	5. CI matrix entry for the project; e2e runs nightly + on PRs touching the
49	   project.
50	6. Traceability matrix entries for every REQ in the project.
51	
52	## Exit criteria
53	
54	- Every REQ in the project is `tests-green` at its declared tier.
55	- E2E tier produces a passing run against the deployed GAS code at least
56	  once on the merge commit to main.
57	- The project's Epic reaches `EPIC.status = done`.
58	- No principle violation surfaced in PHASE-5 was resolved by a workaround;
59	  each was either fixed by an ADR amendment or remains an open ADR-amendment
60	  proposal blocking promotion to `prod`.
61	
62	## Out of scope
63	
64	- Production deployment promotion (separate `gate`-type task on the merged
65	  PR).
66	- Additional projects (each subsequent project is its own PR chain following
67	  the same template).
68	
69	## Exit gate
70	
71	`phase-exit` verifies project REQs tests-green, Epic done, ≥ 1 nightly E2E
72	green, no unresolved principle violations.

content.text:
Now I have read all 15 documents. I have enough context to produce the structural governance review.

STRUCTGOV-CRIT-001 | ARCHITECTURE.md §3 (lines 47-107) vs CLAUDE.md §3 vs ADR-0001 §0 / §0 / §0 vs ADR-0002 §2. ARCHITECTURE.md §3 documents the BMAD install path as `_bmad/` (underscore-prefixed) and the BMAD-output staging dir as `openspec/_bmad-output/`, matching ADR-0002 amendment 0001. But ADR-0001 §0 "Decision" rules (line 46) still names the BMAD install as `bmad/` ("`bmad/` is the BMAD installation, treated as upstream-managed"), and PHASE-1 §Scope item 3 (line 31) similarly says "`bmad/` installation per ADR-0002, pinned in `bmad/config.yaml`" (an artifact that ADR-0002 §1 contradicts; the pin lives in `_bmad/_config/manifest.yaml`). ARCHITECTURE.md §9 (line 215) and PHASE-1 §Scope items 4 (line 34) still describe a wrapper layer under `.claude/agents/` and `.claude/commands/` that ADR-0002 amendment 0001 explicitly retired. Governance consequence: there are now at least three structurally equal places naming the BMAD substrate with no canonical resolver. A downstream reviewer can cite ARCHITECTURE.md to demand wrappers, cite ADR-0002 to forbid them, and cite ADR-0001 to refuse the rename — none of the documents tells them which wins. Amendment-driven retro-edits to ADR-0002 left every sibling document stale.

STRUCTGOV-CRIT-002 | ADR-0002 sectioning (§§1-9 + Amendment log). The ADR mixes original-revision content with amendment-introduced sections under a single header sequence: §§1-6 are original (per Amendment 0001 in the log "Complete rewrite"), §7-§9 are introduced by Amendment 0002, but the section headings carry no marker distinguishing them from original text except a parenthetical "(Amendment 0002.)" inline. There is no separately-addressable amendment block. Governance consequence: an auditor asking "what did Amendment 0002 actually change?" cannot diff structurally — they must regex-scan for the parenthetical. Worse, future amendments that further modify §7-§9 will overwrite Amendment 0002 text in place with no way to attribute the post-state to a specific decision; this breaks the append-only-at-the-assertion-level discipline ADR-0004 §7 mandates for spec REQs but does not extend to ADRs.

STRUCTGOV-CRIT-003 | ADR-0002 §7 (line 67). The section is numbered "## 7." but the preceding section is "6. Upgrade procedure" (line 56) under the "## Decision" header, while §7 is at top level. So §7-§9 are siblings of `## Context`, `## Decision`, `## Consequences`, `## Compliance`, `## Amendment log` — not subsections of `## Decision` as their numbering (continuing from `Decision`'s internal 1-6) implies. Governance consequence: the numbering implies a containment hierarchy that does not exist in the heading tree. Tooling that indexes ADRs by markdown heading level (e.g., `gate-coverage` walking ADRs per ADR-0008 §2) will see §7-§9 as Decision-peers, but a human auditing decision attribution will read them as Decision children. The two readings imply different governance scopes for these rules.

STRUCTGOV-SER-001 | ADR-0005 §10 (line 183) numbered "## 10. Amendment log" vs ADR-0002 trailing "## Amendment log" (line 200, unnumbered). Two ADRs adopt incompatible conventions for the same governance artifact in the same directory. Governance consequence: amendment-log discovery cannot be mechanized by section number or by stable anchor. An "amendments since 2026-01" report has to fall back to fuzzy heading matching, defeating ADR-0004 §2's stable-reference discipline at the ADR's own decision-record layer.

STRUCTGOV-SER-002 | ADR-0004 §5 (line 77 storage format) vs ADR-0005 §3 (line 51 commit trailers) vs ADR-0008 §1 (gate inventory). ADR-0004 §5 defines per-REQ frontmatter fields (`status`, `phase`, `tier`, `references`). ADR-0005 §3 defines per-commit trailers (`Task`, `Requirements`, `Tests-Status`, `Phase`). The two carry overlapping fields (status/Tests-Status, phase/Phase) but no cross-reference declares which side is canonical when they disagree. ADR-0008's gates name neither side as authoritative. Governance consequence: a committer choosing between updating frontmatter or updating the trailer has no rule to follow; the matrix builder (ADR-0005 §2) regenerates from both sources without a defined precedence, making conflict resolution implicit in tool code rather than in spec.

STRUCTGOV-SER-003 | ARCHITECTURE.md §10 (lines 220-244) "Architecture requirements (REQ-ARCH-*)". REQ-ARCH-0001 through 0008 are defined inside ARCHITECTURE.md itself, as level-3-content prose bullets, *not* as ADR-0004 §5 REQ blocks with YAML frontmatter. ADR-0004 §5 mandates the REQ block format universally; ADR-0004 §0 Context says specifications are "addressed by stable identifiers and file paths." But the REQ-ARCH set has no frontmatter, no `tier`, no `revision`, no `references` — they cannot be ingested by the same matrix builder that ADR-0005 §2 specifies. Governance consequence: the architecture's own exit-criteria REQs are not structurally addressable by the traceability tooling that the architecture mandates. PHASE-0 §Exit (lines 48-57) says these must be `tests-green in the matrix`, but the matrix builder has no schema to read them from. The repository's foundation REQs are governance-orphans.

STRUCTGOV-SER-004 | ARCHITECTURE.md §4 (line 116) Atomic units table omits `Hook` and `CI Gate`. ADR-0005 §6 introduces hooks as named entities; ADR-0008 §1 introduces gates with stable kebab-case names (e.g., `matrix-drift`, `phase-exit`); ADR-0008 §2 makes `gate-coverage` a self-walking enforcement. Neither gates nor hooks appear in the atomic-units identifier table. Governance consequence: when a gate is referenced across documents, there is no stable ID format and no canonical home for the gate's definition — `gate-coverage` is referenced four times across ADR-0008 and the phase files by lowercase name only, with no anchor. A future amendment removing or renaming a gate has no immutable identifier to supersede; the rename is silent.

STRUCTGOV-SER-005 | ADR-0008 §1 table (lines 23-50) uses kebab-case identifiers (`matrix-drift`, `red-before-green`) while ADR-0004 uses ALL-CAPS hyphenated rule names referenced as `prose-xref-banned` (lower) and the storage tag `top-level-allowlist`. ADR-0005 §6 hook column uses snake_case paths (`tools/trace/validate_commit.py`). ARCHITECTURE.md §4 enforces ALL-CAPS for atomic units. Governance consequence: four naming conventions coexist in one architectural artifact, with no convention table defining which applies where. A new gate's author has no rule for what casing to use; reviewers have no rule to enforce against.

STRUCTGOV-SER-006 | ADR-0008 §1 cross-references (e.g., "ADR-0004 §1", "ADR-0005 §6") in the "Enforces" column. References use ADR-ID + section number but not file path, violating ARCHITECTURE.md §4 line 135 and ADR-0004 §2 line 46 ("Every cross-reference uses ID plus file path"). The Owner column references `tools/spec_lint` etc. without ID format. Governance consequence: the very gate that enforces cross-reference discipline (`xref-resolves`, ADR-0008 §1 row) lives in a table whose references are themselves non-conforming. Either the rule does not apply to ADRs (in which case the discipline has an unwritten scope carve-out) or the canonical example of gate documentation is itself in violation — both readings undermine `prose-xref-banned`'s legitimacy.

STRUCTGOV-SER-007 | PHASE files (PHASE-0 through PHASE-5) carry frontmatter with `id`, `title`, `status`, `entry` — but no `exit` field, no `requires_adrs[]`, no `produces_reqs[]`. The actual exit criteria and entry dependencies live in markdown prose body sections. Governance consequence: phase status (the `phase-exit` gate per ADR-0008 §3) must be computed by parsing prose bullet lists rather than from structured frontmatter. PHASE-2's exit (lines 67-77) references "all gates in ADR-0008 §1 not owned by `tools/spec_lint`" as a prose expression — no enumeration tooling can derive the set without resolving the cross-document predicate. The phase-gate is structurally non-mechanical.

STRUCTGOV-SER-008 | PHASE-5 §Scope item 4 (line 36) is the second item numbered "4." after item 4 "Per-project BMAD output isolation"; the original `4. Test suite across all four tiers` (line 47) follows. There are now two §Scope-4 items in the same phase file. Governance consequence: an exit-criteria audit referencing "PHASE-5 §Scope 4" is ambiguous. The list-number drift was introduced by an Amendment 0002 add that did not renumber; the structural collision is the same class of defect as STRUCTGOV-CRIT-002 (amendment overlay without separable addressing), here surfacing inside a phase file.

STRUCTGOV-SER-009 | ARCHITECTURE.md §11 (line 247) "Phased delivery" table is the canonical phase summary, but each phase file's frontmatter `entry:` is restated in prose (e.g., PHASE-2 line 5 "entry: PHASE-1 exit criteria all tests-green"). Neither side is declared as the SoT. Governance consequence: a phase rename or reordering must be applied in two places. ARCHITECTURE.md §10 REQ-ARCH-0002 enumerates PHASE-0..PHASE-5 by ID — a third place. Cross-doc consistency is now hand-maintained across three surfaces with no lint rule covering them (`xref-resolves` only checks link existence, not value equality).

STRUCTGOV-SER-010 | ADR-0005 §6 hook table (lines 93-101) and §10 Amendment log. The hook for `PostToolUse(mcp__github__merge_pull_request)` and the extended push semantics are introduced by amendment 0001, but the §6 table row reads as if it were original — the amendment is identified only in §10's text "Adds `PostToolUse(mcp__github__merge_pull_request)` hook." A naïve reader of §6 cannot distinguish original from amended rows. Governance consequence: future amendments cannot reliably address a single row of the §6 table — Amendment 0002 saying "modify the PostToolUse(merge_pull_request) hook" has no stable anchor to cite. Hook governance is by table, but tables are not addressable.

STRUCTGOV-SER-011 | Directory layout in ARCHITECTURE.md §3 places `openspec/specs/` (authored) and `openspec/traceability/matrix.yaml` (derived per ADR-0005 §2) at sibling-equal positions under `openspec/`. The "derived cache" status is annotated in a code-comment ("# derived cache; truth = journals + git + specs + tests"), not in directory structure. Governance consequence: a tool walking `openspec/` to enumerate authored artifacts cannot mechanically distinguish authored from derived. `INDEX.yaml` (also derived) sits inside `openspec/specs/` next to authored spec files; `_bmad-output/` (staging/non-authoritative) sits at the same level as authored briefs/prd. The layout offers no segregation that governance tooling can enforce via directory glob; every tool must hardcode a denylist.

STRUCTGOV-SER-012 | ARCHITECTURE.md §1 (lines 17-34) "Operating principles" lists the five principles inline as a numbered list, with no stable IDs (P1..P5 are used as anchors in ADRs — ADR-0003 line 15 references "P1–P5", ADR-0006 line 14 references "Principle P3", ADR-0007 line 13 references "Principle P2") but those anchors are not declared in ARCHITECTURE.md §1 itself. Governance consequence: ADRs reference principles by an ID format (P1..P5) that the canonical source does not define. A rename or reordering of the principle list would silently invalidate every ADR's cross-reference, and `xref-resolves` (link-based) cannot detect the drift. The most-cited governance artifacts in the repo have no stable IDs.

STRUCTGOV-SER-013 | CLAUDE.md §"Operating principles" claims to restate ARCHITECTURE.md §1 "verbatim" (per REQ-ARCH-0007 line 238). The two texts are structurally identical but CLAUDE.md adds a sixth sentence ("A principle without a CI gate is a build failure"). That sentence is sourced from ADR-0008 §0 Context. Governance consequence: REQ-ARCH-0007 (line 238) demands verbatim equality of the principle list, but CLAUDE.md mixes principle text with ADR-0008-derived enforcement text in the same numbered block. A future test of REQ-ARCH-0007 cannot be defined without a structural separator distinguishing principle from enforcement clause. Two governance artifacts (principles, gate-coverage) coexist in one paragraph with no boundary.

STRUCTGOV-PROC-001 | ADR file naming convention `ADR-NNNN-kebab-title.md` vs PHASE file naming `PHASE-N-kebab-title.md`. Both encode the ID in the filename but no rule (in ADR-0004 or elsewhere) declares the filename pattern authoritative. ARCHITECTURE.md §4 line 124 "Canonical home" for ADR is "openspec/architecture/decisions/" — directory, not filename pattern. Governance consequence: a renamed ADR file (e.g., `ADR-0002-bmad-integration.md` → `ADR-0002-bmad.md`) would break every cross-reference but would not violate any documented structural rule; the convention is enforced only by social convention and by the fact that current xrefs happen to match.

STRUCTGOV-PROC-002 | ADR `Compliance` sections (present in ADR-0001 §Compliance, ADR-0002, ADR-0003, ADR-0004, ADR-0005, ADR-0006, ADR-0007, ADR-0008) list lint rules and CI gates that enforce the decision — but the same gate is sometimes declared in two ADRs' Compliance blocks (e.g., `top-level-allowlist` appears in ADR-0001 §Compliance and ADR-0002 §Compliance, while ADR-0008 §1 lists it once as enforcing ADR-0001). Governance consequence: which ADR "owns" each gate is multivalued. When `top-level-allowlist` needs amendment, three ADRs may need synchronized updates and no rule names the primary. The Compliance-section / gate-inventory split is two coordinate systems for the same fact with no canonical resolver.

STRUCTGOV-PROC-003 | ADR `Amendment log` is present only in ADR-0002 and ADR-0005. ADRs 0001, 0003, 0004, 0006, 0007, 0008 have no amendment-log section at all. The append-only-amendment discipline ADR-0002 §Amendment log line 207 declares ("Amendments are append-only. A subsequent material change to behavior introduced by a prior amendment requires a new amendment row, never an edit to a prior row.") is local to that ADR and not promulgated. Governance consequence: when ADR-0004 is amended, there is no declared structural mechanism — the amender will either invent a section (creating a fourth convention) or edit-in-place (silently destroying the prior-state record). The amendment discipline is artifact-local rather than directory-wide.

STRUCTGOV-PROC-004 | ADR-0003 §3 (line 42) names four test tiers (unit/integration/e2e/stochastic). ADR-0006 §1 (line 20) names the same four tiers in a slightly different table. ADR-0007 §1 (line 23) describes the stochastic tier. ARCHITECTURE.md §3 line 104 shows the layout `tests/{unit,integration,e2e,stochastic}/`. Four declarations of the tier set with no SoT pointer. Governance consequence: adding a fifth tier (the ADR-0006 §Consequences line 89 explicitly anticipates `chaos`) requires synchronized edits across four documents. Cross-doc tier consistency is hand-maintained.

STRUCTGOV-PROC-005 | PHASE-1 §Scope (lines 27-39) and PHASE-2 §Scope (lines 22-65) both reference BMAD/spec-lint deliverables that ADR-0002 §Compliance line 184 and ADR-0008 §1 also enumerate. The same gate (e.g., `skill-removal-acknowledged`) appears in ADR-0002 §Compliance, ADR-0008 §1 (it is in fact absent there — verify), and PHASE-2 §Scope item 4. Governance consequence: PHASE files redundantly enumerate gate sets defined in ADR-0008; when ADR-0008 §1 grows a gate, PHASE files do not auto-acquire it. Phase-scope/gate-inventory drift is unenforced. (And spot-check: `skill-removal-acknowledged` is in ADR-0002 §Compliance and PHASE-2 §Scope but does *not* appear in ADR-0008 §1 — a concrete cross-document drift already manifest.)

STRUCTGOV-PROC-006 | ADR-0002 §Amendment log row 0002 (line 205) cites "CHG-0010 (TASK-0021)" while row 0001 (line 204) cites only "CHG-0010". Two amendment rows on the same change use different reference granularity. ADR-0005 §10 row 0001 cites "CHG-0002" with no task. Governance consequence: amendment traceability has no schema. A query "show all amendments resulting from TASK-0021" cannot be answered by parsing amendment logs — task-granularity is optional and inconsistent. ADR-0005 §3's mandatory `Task: TASK-NNNN` trailer demands task-level attribution for commits but the amendment log (a higher-status artifact) does not.

Summary: 3 CRIT, 13 SER, 6 PROC findings = 22 total. The dominant structural pattern undermining governance is **amendment-overlay without separable addressing** — amendments to ADR-0002 and ADR-0005 retro-fit new sections and table rows into documents whose original numbering, layout, and cross-references were already cited by sibling artifacts; nothing in the structure marks original-vs-amended content, and nothing forces sibling documents (ARCHITECTURE.md, PHASE files, peer ADRs) to re-sync. The result is silent drift between the amended ADR and every consumer of its pre-amendment state, with no canonical resolver to cite when consumers disagree. STRUCTGOV-CRIT-001/002/003, SER-001/008/010, and PROC-003/006 are all instances of this pattern; together they make the architecture's own amendment lifecycle the largest source of governance-erosive structure in the artifact set.
